title: Decompiled ned.exe (NED32.py) — Fernet ransomware targeting MAC `00:0C:29:EB:E8:84`
html:
The reverse pipeline worked — Yahir is now staring at uncompyle6 output of NED32.py (decompiled from PyInstaller-packed ned.exe). It's a Fernet-key ransomware PoC: imports cryptography.fernet, os, winapi, getmac. Hardcoded target MAC 00:0C:29:EB:E8:84 (a VMware-prefix address, so it's environment-locked to a specific lab guest). encrypt() walks os.walk('/'), encrypts every .txt/.docx/.png/.jpg/.jpeg/.csv/.xlsx/.xls/.pdf with Fernet.encrypt(...), renames each to *.enc, then drops a ransom note. enumDrives() enumerates Windows logical drives via win32api.GetLogicalDriveStrings(). The deliberate skip-list excludes 2024-01-02-Re-Organization_Meeting.pdf — likely the lure document.

TL;DR

  • ned.exe is a Fernet-based ransomware PoC — encrypts every doc/image, renames to .enc, drops a ransom note per directory.
  • MAC address 00:0C:29:EB:E8:84 is hard-coded as the targeted host — explicitly environment-locked, which is why this is a teaching artifact, not a worm.
  • Skip-list keeps 2024-01-02-Re-Organization_Meeting.pdf intact — that's the social-engineering lure file the user opened.
  • This is the smoking-gun answer for the OSU coursework writeup at "targeted attack against financial security mechanisms".

malware analysis lab · network forensics · python · wireshark · osu
